nativeRange.habitat: Warm, nutrient-rich, still or slow-moving freshwater — Amazon-basin oxbow lakes, lagoons, backwater channels, marshes, and the quiet margins of lowland rivers; floats freely on the surface in full sun, often forming dense interlocking mats

decorPreferences: open, calm water surface for the floating rosette and its long trailing roots
open-top tank or pond — emergent leaves rise up to ~30 cm above the waterline and need headroom
strong overhead light to keep growth compact and prevent the rosette from stretching and rotting
low surface agitation — sustained spray or splash wets the bulbous petioles and leaves and causes rot

goodWith: outdoor ponds and tubs in jurisdictions where the plant is legal
livebearer fry and small fish that shelter in the trailing root mass
labyrinth fish (gouramis, bettas) that anchor bubble-nests to the underside
high-bioload tanks needing aggressive nitrate export
avoidWith: any outdoor body of water connected to natural waterways — escape risk is severe and dispersal causes ecosystem collapse
low-light or indoor tanks without strong direct lighting (plant etiolates and rots)
tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars, or overhead drip
goldfish, large herbivorous cichlids, and other vigorous plant-eaters

imageCandidates.notes: Close-up of the species' signature lavender-purple flower spike — strongest visual identifier for Pontederia crassipes / Eichhornia crassipes. URL pattern follows commons naming; reviewer should confirm exact file before upload.
Product page hosts close-range photos of the bulbous-petiole rosette as sold to hobbyists; useful reference but not directly reusable. Reviewer should source a CC-licensed image instead.

summary: Water Hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes, modern Pontederia crassipes) is a free-floating South American plant with glossy rounded leaves on inflated, air-filled petioles and showy lavender-purple flower spikes. One of the fastest-growing plants on Earth — and one of the most destructive invasive aquatic weeds worldwide.
careNotes: Husbandry is trivial — full sun or strong aquarium light, warm water (20-30 °C optimum, will survive briefly down to ~12 °C), no CO2, no substrate, and no fertilization beyond ambient tank nutrients. The plant thrives on dissolved nitrogen and phosphorus and is exceptional at stripping nutrients from high-bioload systems, which is why it has historically been used in pond filtration, livestock-wastewater polishing, and koi-pond shade. Growth is explosive: mats double in 1-2 weeks under good conditions, and the published literature cites populations multiplying more than a hundredfold in 23 days. Indoors, the practical problem is the opposite — without genuinely strong direct light, the bulbous petioles elongate (etiolate) and the rosette collapses and rots, so most aquarium-grown specimens decline within weeks unless given pond-grade light. Tank fit: emergent leaves rise 15-30 cm above the waterline and the trailing root mass can reach 30-50 cm below, so plan on an open-top tank of roughly 110 L (~30 gal) or larger, or an outdoor tub/pond. Avoid sustained surface agitation: persistently wet leaves and petioles rot at the base. The most important note is non-husbandry: Eichhornia crassipes is one of the world's worst invasive aquatic plants and is a federally listed noxious weed in the United States (regulated by USDA APHIS), banned by the European Union since 2016 (EU Regulation 1143/2014 list of invasive alien species of Union concern), prohibited under New Zealand's National Pest Plant Accord, and restricted in much of Australia and across Africa and Asia. Within the US, even where federal commerce is now permitted, individual states including Alabama, Arizona, Florida, Idaho, Mississippi, Nebraska, South Carolina, Texas, and Wisconsin prohibit sale or possession. Never release into any waterway, storm drain, or compost pile with runoff potential — dispose of excess plants by drying to brittleness and bagging for landfill.
breedingNotes: Reproduces almost entirely by vegetative stolons in cultivation — a parent rosette extends horizontal runners that bud off identical daughter plantlets, which detach when the runner decays. This is the mechanism behind the species' notorious doubling time. Sexual reproduction via the lavender flower spikes is possible (8-15 flowers per spike, six tepals, central yellow-and-purple 'peacock' marking), produces wind- and water-dispersed seeds that remain viable in sediment for 15-20 years, and is a major reason eradication efforts repeatedly fail in invaded waters. For aquarium and pond keepers, separate any daughter rosette once it develops its own root mass.

primary.name: Wikipedia — Pontederia crassipes
primary.notes: Authoritative taxonomy reference: family Pontederiaceae, order Commelinales, modern reclassification from Eichhornia crassipes to Pontederia crassipes (subgenus Oshunae). Provides native range (South American Amazon basin), temperature tolerance (min 12 °C, optimum 25-30 °C, max 33-35 °C), pH 5.0-7.5, salinity ceiling (~15 % seawater), leaf size 10-20 cm, plant height up to ~1 m, flower description (lavender-pink spikes of 8-15 flowers), explosive growth (mat doubling in 1-2 weeks, >100x multiplication in 23 days), and the invasive-status timeline including EU 2016 ban, NZ National Pest Plant Accord, and the 1956-2020 US federal ban history.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Hobby-side cross-check: lighting moderate-to-strong (strong light drives very fast growth), CO2 not necessary, temperature 68-80 °F (20-27 °C), growth very fast, care level very easy, placement floating in ponds/tubs/open-top tanks/sumps, arrival width 7-8 inches (~18-20 cm), recommends 30+ gallon (~110 L) tank or pond. Crucially confirms current US shipping restrictions: 'does not ship to Alabama, Arizona, Florida, Idaho, Mississippi, Nebraska, South Carolina, Texas, or Wisconsin' — direct evidence for the legally-restricted hobbyist advisory.

propagation: seed from lavender flower spikes (viable 15-20 years in sediment; relevant for invasive spread, not aquarium propagation)

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Eichhornia crassipes (Pontederia crassipes) is one of the world's worst invasive aquatic plants. It is a US federally listed noxious weed regulated by USDA APHIS, banned across the European Union (EU Regulation 1143/2014, listed 2016), prohibited under New Zealand's National Pest Plant Accord, restricted across much of Australia, and barred from sale or possession in numerous US states including Alabama, Arizona, Florida, Idaho, Mississippi, Nebraska, South Carolina, Texas, and Wisconsin. Check local law before acquiring, and never release plants into any waterway, storm drain, or runoff-exposed compost.
